tweets.text:
From last Sunday's Week Ahead.  Sub here and you'll get the next report this Sunday: https://t.co/q0o89SN2xg

While there has been a lot of discussion about Q1 earnings (with some focused on the boost from non-cash benefits including hyperscaler investments in other AI companies, I feel that we have somewhat glossed over that revenues (not so lifted by one-time items) grew 11.8% in Q1, the best since the inflation boosted Q2 2022 results (13.9%) led by Tech (+31.7%) and Comm Services +15.1%.

So there was plenty of sales growth to go along with those earnings.

tweets.text:
BoA from three weeks ago: The longer the war continues, the closer the midterms, and the more likely it becomes that higher gas prices have an impact. This could affect the US Administration's calculus around the war. But it also likely factors into Iran's thinking, as delaying any agreement could deter potential future strikes. 

Although our base case remains a resolution in the next few weeks, markets could be underpricing risks of a more extended conflict. 

Besides the war, the midterms are also likely to impact the calculus around trade policy. With Section 122 tariffs expiring no later than July, the US Administration is expected to announce additional tariffs under Sections 232 and 301. However, we think the decline in tariff revenues that we have already observed since the SCOTUS ruling on IEEPA could continue. We see effective tariffs likely to end up in the 6-8% range, though the latest escalation with the EU poses some upside risks to this view.

Goldman: Our analysis suggests that AI-related spending will boost true capex growth [in GDP] by about 3.3pp in 2026. 

However, as we noted previously, BEA currently treats semiconductors as intermediate inputs and therefore excludes them from the investment component. This means that the impact of AI on measured capex growth is likely smaller—roughly 2.8pp.

BoA (Hartnett): The stock of US private sector financial  assets (stocks & bonds, but also cash deposits, loans, private equity, and pension fund reserves) is [at a record high] relative to US GDP, illustrating the “financialization” of  the US economy and the high degree of wealth inequality.

The value of financial assets (Wall St)  relative to GDP (Main St) is currently  6.5x…a new high, well above the range of  2.5x-3.5x in the 30–40-year period prior to  the globalization & deregulation of the 1980s & early 1990s.

tweets.text:
JPM (Feroli): A cumulative 3.1pt slump in the DM services PMI output since January contrasts with a 1.2pt rise in manufacturing (Figure 2).

While momentum swings in manufacturing often represent the forward edge of the business cycle, the recent sharp slide in services could point to a broad turn towards business and household caution. 

Similarly, the resilience of U.S. survey results contrasts sharply with Western Europe's weakness is usually a reliable signal for the direction of the global business cycle. 

But with a US purchasing power cushion from tax cuts likely to prove transitory and the rebound in US business sentiment and hiring still in its early stages, the warning signals coming from Europe should not be ignored.

Goldman's Analyst Index (basically a PMI based on input from their analysts) increased by 7.8pt to 66.4 in May to the highest level since May 2022. The composition was strong, as the new orders (+21.2pt to 77.7), employment (+6.0pt to 51.2), and shipments (+3.0pt to 63.9) components all increased. 

The GSAI’s output prices component increased to its highest level since June 2021.

tweets.text:
BoA: With the Fed conversation moving from cuts to hikes, there has been renewed interest  in the Taylor Rule. 

The Fed looks at a range of Taylor Rule models that assess how policy should be set in response to inflation and activity data. The two most important models are: i) the original Taylor Rule from 1993 and ii) the balanced approach, which doubles the weight on the activity side of the mandate. 

We replicate these models using the unemployment gap (the equilibrium u-rate minus the actual u-rate) instead of the output gap, and adjusting the corresponding weight based on Okun’s Law [which posits when unemployment increases by ~1% GDP falls by ~2% and vice versa]. For the neutral real rate, the equilibrium u-rate and the inflation  objective, we use the median longer run estimates from the Fed’s SEP. We assume the  target variable for inflation is y/y core PCE.

Exhibit 1 shows that Fed policy was in line with the 1993 Taylor Rule and Balanced Approach models for most of last year. Policy now looks loose again, due to i) a pickup in  core PCE inflation, ii) a decline in the u-rate relative to 4Q 2025, and iii) a small uptick in the Fed’s median longer run policy rate estimate. Taken at face value, both models call  for about 100bp of hikes.
tweets.full_text:
BoA: With the Fed conversation moving from cuts to hikes, there has been renewed interest  in the Taylor Rule. 

The Fed looks at a range of Taylor Rule models that assess how policy should be set in response to inflation and activity data. The two most important models are: i) the original Taylor Rule from 1993 and ii) the balanced approach, which doubles the weight on the activity side of the mandate. 

We replicate these models using the unemployment gap (the equilibrium u-rate minus the actual u-rate) instead of the output gap, and adjusting the corresponding weight based on Okun’s Law [which posits when unemployment increases by ~1% GDP falls by ~2% and vice versa]. For the neutral real rate, the equilibrium u-rate and the inflation  objective, we use the median longer run estimates from the Fed’s SEP. We assume the  target variable for inflation is y/y core PCE.

Exhibit 1 shows that Fed policy was in line with the 1993 Taylor Rule and Balanced Approach models for most of last year. Policy now looks loose again, due to i) a pickup in  core PCE inflation, ii) a decline in the u-rate relative to 4Q 2025, and iii) a small uptick in the Fed’s median longer run policy rate estimate. Taken at face value, both models call  for about 100bp of hikes.

tweets.text:
Goldman: US IPO activity has recently accelerated following four consecutive years of muted issuance. 

24 US companies have come to market so far in Q2 2026, bringing the YTD total to 40. This represents more than double the number of IPOs at this point in 2025 and the largest total through May since the 95 IPOs launched in the first five months of 2021. 

Nonetheless, despite the recent pickup in activity, the number of deals so far this year has lagged the average total of 50 IPOs through May since 1995.

Similarly, US IPOs have raised $28 billion in gross proceeds YTD, the largest total at this point in the year since 2021 (which ended with a record $115 billion).
tweets.full_text:
Goldman: US IPO activity has recently accelerated following four consecutive years of muted issuance.

Nonetheless, despite the recent pickup in activity, the number of deals so far this year has lagged the average total of 50 IPOs through May since 1995.

Similarly, US IPOs have raised $28 billion in gross proceeds YTD, the largest total at this point in the year since 2021 (which ended with a record $115 billion).

tweets.text:
Rubner on retail flows: 

The key difference versus earlier this year is where that participation has gone. Rather than concentrating in speculative meme-style activity, retail has increasingly piled into mega-cap tech and semiconductors – precisely the areas that have experienced the most historic upside moves.

Citadel Securities gross retail cash volumes ranked in the 96th percentile in April versus all months since 2019, while May is currently on track to become the most active month ever, pacing roughly ~12% above January 2021 levels. 

Options activity has been similarly impressive, with April posting the second-highest ADV on record (behind Oct’2025) and May currently tracking toward a new high, with average daily contracts traded pacing ~60% above the historical monthly average.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Citadel's Rubner: The current equity rally remains characterized by a powerful but increasingly unusual combination: historically narrow breadth alongside exceptionally strong earnings growth. Rather than a speculative bubble driven purely by multiple expansion, mega-cap https://t.co/12nCRUdQyK
